summary: Gruber surveys notable updates in the Mac text editor landscape. TextWrangler 1.5 adds full AppleScript support and expanded syntax coloring, significantly closing the gap with its pricier sibling BBEdit. He highlights Michael Tsai's BBAutoComplete, a free auto-completion tool that works across multiple scriptable editors via Apple events. He also notes the collaborative editor Hydra's update with WebKit-powered live HTML previews, while lamenting its forced name change due to a legal dispute.
key_points: TextWrangler 1.5's addition of AppleScript support is a major upgrade that moves it much closer to BBEdit's capabilities for just $49
AppleScript support matters even for non-scripters because it enables third-party tools like BBAutoComplete to communicate with editors via Apple events
BBAutoComplete's on-demand approach to completion is superior to editors that automatically suggest completions as you type
Hydra's Rendezvous-based collaborative editing and WebKit live previews represent cutting-edge features, though the editor faces a forced name change
money_quotes: Using a scriptable text editor can be incredibly useful — even if you never write a single script yourself.
The addition of AppleScript support is huge.

Many people mistakenly felt that TextWrangler 1.0 was little more than a renamed commercial version of the free BBEdit Lite; the addition of full scriptability clearly moves TextWrangler's capabilities much closer to BBEdit's.
I despise auto-completing editors which automatically pop-up or flash suggested completions on-the-fly as you type. It's like trying to write while someone else is typing in the same window.
Regardless if you like BBAutoComplete's style of auto-completion, you can't argue with the fact that it wouldn't be possible if the aforementioned editors didn't offer terrific AppleScript support.
And of course if you really do want multiple authors typing in the same window simultaneously, Hydra has been updated to version 1.1.2.
key_insight: Scriptability in text editors isn't just a power-user feature — it's the foundation that enables an entire ecosystem of third-party tools and workflows, making AppleScript support a transformative addition to any editor.
